profile.name: Col. Jesse Marcel Jr.
roles: Otolaryngologist (ENT Physician) - private practice, Helena, MT
Colonel - Montana Army National Guard (Flight Surgeon)
profile.summary: Otolaryngologist and Montana Army National Guard Colonel who, as an 11-year-old in July 1947, handled debris his father - RAAF intelligence officer Jesse Marcel Sr. - brought home from the Foster Ranch crash site before reporting to base. Marcel Jr. consistently described the material as exhibiting anomalous properties unlike any terrestrial technology, including memory-metal characteristics and geometric symbols on structural members. He co-authored 'The Roswell Legacy' (2009) and testified at the Citizen Hearing on Disclosure weeks before his death in 2013. As both a childhood firsthand witness and a credentialed military-medical professional, he represents the most direct family corroboration of his father's account.
education: M.D., Louisiana State University School of Medicine, 1961
early_career: Established private ENT practice in Helena, Montana, where he practiced for most of his adult life
Commissioned into the Montana Army National Guard; eventually attained the rank of Colonel serving as a flight surgeon
Deployed to Iraq 2004-2005 as a flight surgeon despite being in his late 60s - a decision he attributed in part to a sense of duty his father had instilled

key_events.event: Age 11: father Jesse Marcel Sr. (RAAF intelligence officer) brought debris from the Foster Ranch crash site home to Houma before reporting to base. Marcel Jr. and his mother handled the material, which he later described as exhibiting memory-metal properties, unusual lightness, and I-beam-like structural members with symbols he could not identify.
Earned his M.D. and established a private otolaryngology practice in Helena, Montana, where he practiced for the next five decades alongside ongoing military service.

Stanton Friedman's landmark interview with Jesse Marcel Sr. brought renewed national attention to the Marcel family's account. Jesse Jr. began sharing his own childhood recollections publicly, corroborating his father's description of anomalous debris properties.

Deployed to Iraq as a flight surgeon with the Montana Army National Guard (Colonel); continued advocacy for Roswell disclosure before and after a deployment that many found remarkable given his age at the time.

Published 'The Roswell Legacy: The Untold Story of the First Military Officer at the Scene of the UFO Crash' with Linda Marcel Zimmermann; the first book-length account of the Marcel family's Roswell experience, incorporating both his father's record and his own childhood testimony.

Testified at the Citizen Hearing on Disclosure at the National Press Club in Washington, D.C.; gave congressional-style testimony before former members of Congress describing his 1947 experience. One of his final public appearances before his death three months later.

Died in Helena, Montana, age 76. His account - maintained without material change from 1978 through his final months - represents over 35 years of consistent firsthand testimony regarding the 1947 Roswell debris.

claims.claim: The debris his father brought home in July 1947 had physical properties unlike any known terrestrial material, including memory-metal characteristics and unidentifiable symbols on structural members
claims.explanation: Marcel Jr.'s description is internally consistent across 35 years of public testimony and matches accounts from his father and other early witnesses. However, no physical material from the Roswell site has ever been independently tested or authenticated. Without recoverable physical evidence, the claim cannot be verified or definitively refuted. The consistency and stability of his account over decades is considered a positive credibility indicator; the absence of physical evidence is the core limitation.

claims.claim: His father's account that the Roswell debris was not a weather balloon or radar reflector is accurate
claims.explanation: Jesse Marcel Sr.'s credibility is well-established - he was a decorated RAAF intelligence officer with 468 combat hours. Both his account and junior's are consistent and have never been retracted. The USAF's 1994 Project Mogul explanation and 1997 test dummy attribution have been criticized by researchers and former officials as inadequate to explain the totality of reported events. The GAO found that RAAF administrative records from the relevant period were destroyed under undocumented authority. The 'partially-verified' rating reflects the institutional documentation failures and witness credibility, not confirmation of the non-terrestrial hypothesis.

supporting: Credentialed medical professional (M.D., practicing otolaryngologist) with no evident financial motivation to fabricate claims
Account maintained without material change across more than 35 years of public testimony - from 1978 through 2013
Served as a Colonel in the Montana Army National Guard; military service and Iraq deployment reflect continued institutional trust
First-person childhood witness - not a secondhand account; directly handled the material described
Account corroborates and is internally consistent with Jesse Marcel Sr.'s contemporaneous testimony
The GAO's own 1995 investigation confirmed RAAF records from the relevant period were destroyed under undocumented authority, adding institutional credibility to claims of a cover-up
contradicting: Account relies on childhood memory of a 1947 event, raising valid questions about accuracy of specific details recalled decades later
No physical material has ever been produced or independently authenticated; the claim cannot be evaluated against evidence
The USAF's Project Mogul explanation (1994) and test dummy report (1997) offer official alternative explanations, though both have been criticized as incomplete
As a firsthand family member with an emotional stake in his father's reputation, objectivity cannot be assumed
credibility.overall: Jesse Marcel Jr. is a credible witness within the limits of his direct evidence - a childhood firsthand account with no documentary corroboration and no surviving physical material. His professional credentials and decades-long consistency are genuine credibility assets. The absence of any physical evidence remains the fundamental constraint. His testimony is best understood as a consistent, credible personal account that corroborates his father's record rather than as standalone confirmatory evidence.

associated_people.name: Maj. Jesse Marcel Sr.
associated_people.relationship: Jesse Jr.'s primary Roswell connection is his father, who retrieved the Foster Ranch debris and brought a sample home in July 1947. Marcel Jr. spent 35 years publicly corroborating and defending his father's account.

associated_people.name: Stanton Friedman
associated_people.relationship: Friedman's 1978 interview with Jesse Marcel Sr. relaunched the Roswell case and drew Marcel Jr. into public testimony. The two remained in contact throughout Friedman's subsequent investigations.

disclosures.title: Post-Friedman interview public appearances
disclosures.outlet: Various broadcast media and UFO conferences
disclosures.notes: Following Stanton Friedman's landmark 1978 interview with his father, Marcel Jr. began giving interviews corroborating the family account; earliest on-record public testimony about his 1947 childhood experience.

disclosures.title: The Roswell Legacy
disclosures.notes: Co-authored with Linda Marcel Zimmermann. First book-length account of the Marcel family's direct Roswell experience, incorporating both Jesse Marcel Sr.'s intelligence record and Jesse Jr.'s childhood testimony.

disclosures.title: Citizen Hearing on Disclosure - Roswell Testimony
disclosures.interviewer: Former members of the U.S. Congress (Citizen Hearing panel)
disclosures.notes: Congressional-style testimony before a panel of six former members of Congress. Described handling the 1947 debris and the anomalous properties observed. One of his final public appearances before his death in August 2013.

sources.title: The Roswell Legacy (book)
sources.notes: Primary published account (New Page Books, 2009) - co-authored with Linda Marcel Zimmermann

sources.title: Citizen Hearing on Disclosure - Jesse Marcel Jr. Testimony (YouTube)
sources.notes: May 2013 congressional-style testimony at National Press Club

sources.title: Roswell Daily Record - Jesse Marcel Jr. Obituary
sources.notes: August 2013 obituary; confirmation of death in Helena, Montana, age 76

career_connections.relationship: Father brought debris home from the Foster Ranch crash site; Marcel Jr.'s entire Roswell testimony stems from this July 1947 encounter.
Childhood firsthand witness; handled debris retrieved by his father from the Foster Ranch crash site in July 1947.
Friedman's 1978 interview with Marcel Sr. brought Marcel Jr. into the public Roswell disclosure effort.
